{"file_path": "output/invoice-review-memo.md", "content": "# INVOICE REVIEW MEMORANDUM

**PRIVILEGED AND CONFIDENTIAL — PREPARED IN ANTICIPATION OF BILLING DISPUTE RESOLUTION**

| | |
|---|---|
| **To:** | Rachel Dominguez, Associate General Counsel (Designated In-House Counsel); Sandra Whitmore, General Counsel |
| **From:** | Invoice Review / Legal Spend Management, Pinnacle Health Systems, Inc. |
| **Date:** | June 12, 2025 |
| **Re:** | Review of Invoice No. HL-2025-05-PIN — Hargrove & Linden LLP — *Pinnacle Health Systems, Inc. v. MedCore Analytics, LLC*, Case No. 1:24-cv-04187-RWS (N.D. Ga.) — Billing Period May 1–31, 2025 |
| **Invoice Amount Requested:** | $201,822.50 ($193,372.50 attorney fees + $8,450.00 expenses) |
| **Recommended Payable Amount:** | **$147,136.88** ($140,786.88 attorney fees + $6,350.00 expenses) |
| **Total Recommended Reduction:** | **$54,685.62** from the amount requested (and $76,993.12 from the gross line-item total) |

---

## I. Executive Summary

This memorandum reviews Invoice No. HL-2025-05-PIN, submitted by Hargrove & Linden LLP (the "Firm") on June 5, 2025 for legal services rendered during May 1–31, 2025 in the captioned matter. The review applies the Firm's Approved Discovery-Phase Staffing Plan (dated February 10, 2025), Pinnacle's Outside Counsel Billing Guidelines (effective January 1, 2023) (the "Billing Guidelines"), the April 28 / May 1, 2025 staffing correspondence, and the Firm's June 1, 2025 Monthly Case Status Report.

The invoice should not be paid as submitted. The review identifies **fourteen distinct issues**, the most serious of which are:

1. **The invoice does not reconcile internally.** The Time Detail line items actually total **$215,680.00 in attorney fees (483.5 hours)**, but the invoice's Summary page and the Time Detail "SUBTOTAL" row both state **$193,372.50 (422.5 hours)** — an unexplained discrepancy of **$22,307.50 and 61.0 hours**. The invoice therefore fails the Section 9.1 requirement that the summary page and time detail page reconcile, and it cannot be processed until the Firm explains the variance.

2. **Two timekeepers who billed the entire month were never approved.** Nina Barros (mid-level associate, 49.0 hours, $23,275.00) and Priya Sengupta (junior associate, 27.0 hours, $10,125.00) are not on the Approved Staffing Plan. The Firm's own Rate Schedule admits Barros is "Pending Approval" and Sengupta was simply "Added May 2025." The April 28 staffing email requested only Barros, and Pinnacle's May 1 reply expressly deferred approval ("let me review this with Sandra and get back to you"); no approval ever issued. Under Section 4.2, all fees attributable to unapproved timekeepers are subject to reduction or rejection — a combined **$33,400.00**.

3. **The contract attorney was billed above both the approved rate and the rate cap.** Marcus Tate was billed at $210/hr, exceeding both the Approved Staffing Plan rate of $185/hr (pass-through, no markup) and the Section 5.1 cap of $200/hr — a **$3,862.50** overcharge across 154.5 hours.

4. **Block billing is pervasive.** Fifty-three (53) entries among the approved timekeepers combine two or more discrete tasks into a single line, triggering the automatic 25% reduction under Section 5.4 — **$30,220.62**.

5. **A single internal strategy call was billed by five timekeepers and a key deposition by three, both in excess of the Section 4.3 limits** and without the required advance approval — **$3,312.50**.

6. **Travel expense of $2,100.00 is non-reimbursable.** It was incurred for two attorneys to travel to the Firm's own Atlanta office for a deposition taken there; local travel within the Atlanta metropolitan area is not billable under any circumstances (Section 6.4).

After applying all reductions to the line-item fee base of $215,680.00, the recommended payable attorney fees are **$140,786.88**. After expense review, recommended payable expenses are **$6,350.00** (with an additional $3,700.00 flagged for verification). The **recommended total payable is $147,136.88**, a reduction of **$54,685.62** from the $201,822.50 requested.

The invoice should be returned to the Firm with this memorandum's itemized reductions, with payment of $147,136.88 (at most) held pending (a) reconciliation of the $22,307.50 internal discrepancy, (b) documentation for the $3,700.00 of flagged expenses, and (c) the Firm's election to resubmit block-billed and task-misassigned entries with separated time under the cure provisions of Sections 5.4 and 9.2.

---

## II. Documents Reviewed

| Document | Date | Source |
|---|---|---|
| Invoice No. HL-2025-05-PIN (Summary, Time Detail, Expense Detail, Rate Schedule) | June 5, 2025 | Hargrove & Linden LLP |
| Approved Discovery-Phase Staffing Plan | February 10, 2025 | Hargrove & Linden LLP (approved by R. Dominguez and S. Whitmore) |
| Pinnacle Outside Counsel Billing Guidelines (Jan. 1, 2023) | Jan. 1, 2023 | Pinnacle Office of the General Counsel |
| Staffing Addition Request email (K. Cho → R. Dominguez) | April 28, 2025 | Hargrove & Linden LLP |
| Staffing Addition Request reply (R. Dominguez → K. Cho) | May 1, 2025 | Pinnacle Health Systems |
| Monthly Case Status Report — May 2025 | June 1, 2025 | Hargrove & Linden LLP |

---

## III. Threshold Issue — The Invoice Does Not Reconcile Internally (Section 9.1)

Before reaching the substantive reductions, a threshold defect must be flagged. Section 9.1 requires each invoice to include both "a summary page showing total fees by timekeeper (name, role, hours, rate, and amount)" and "a time detail page with individual entries." The two must reconcile. They do not.

The Summary page and the Time Detail "SUBTOTAL" row each state attorney fees of **$193,372.50 (422.5 hours)**. However, the individual line items in the Time Detail actually sum to **$215,680.00 (483.5 hours)** — a discrepancy of **$22,307.50 and 61.0 hours** that the invoice does not explain. The variance is not concentrated in one timekeeper; it appears across most of the team:

| Timekeeper | Summary (hrs / $) | Line Items (hrs / $) | Variance (hrs / $) |
|---|---|---|---|
| David Hargrove | 38.5 / $37,922.50 | 38.5 / $37,922.50 | 0.0 / $0.00 |
| Karen Cho | 74.0 / $46,250.00 | 89.5 / $55,937.50 | +15.5 / +$9,687.50 |
| James Pettersson | 82.5 / $39,187.50 | 91.0 / $43,225.00 | +8.5 / +$4,037.50 |
| Nina Barros | 45.0 / $21,375.00 | 49.0 / $23,275.00 | +4.0 / +$1,900.00 |
| Tyler Webb | 35.0 / $13,125.00 | 34.0 / $12,750.00 | −1.0 / −$375.00 |
| Priya Sengupta | 27.5 / $10,312.50 | 27.0 / $10,125.00 | −0.5 / −$187.50 |
| Marcus Tate | 120.0 / $25,200.00 | 154.5 / $32,445.00 | +34.5 / +$7,245.00 |
| **Total** | **422.5 / $193,372.50** | **483.5 / $215,680.00** | **+61.0 / +$22,307.50** |

**Consequence and recommendation.** Because the line items exceed the stated total, the invoice as presented is internally inconsistent and cannot be paid on its face. The substantive reductions in this memorandum are computed against the **line-item** base of $215,680.00, because the line items are the granular record of the work actually claimed and are where every substantive defect (block billing, task misassignment, etc.) resides. Pinnacle should require the Firm to reconcile the $22,307.50 / 61.0-hour variance before any payment, and should not pay any amount attributable to line items the Firm cannot substantiate. The recommended payable figure below assumes the line-item base is the correct one; if the Firm instead contends the $193,372.50 figure is correct, it must identify and withdraw the excess 61.0 hours of line items.

---

## IV. Findings and Reduction Calculations

### Finding 1 — Unapproved Timekeeper: Nina Barros (Section 4.2)

**Reduction: $23,275.00 (full write-off of 49.0 hours × $475).**

Nina Barros, a fourth-year mid-level associate, is not on the Approved Staffing Plan. The Plan is explicit that "James Pettersson is the only mid-level associate assigned to this matter. No other mid-level associate is included on the approved staffing roster," and that any addition requires "prior written approval" under Section 4.2.

The record shows the Firm attempted to obtain approval but did not succeed. On April 28, 2025, Karen Cho emailed Rachel Dominguez requesting Barros's addition at "$475/hr" for an estimated "40–50 hours per month." Pinnacle's Designated In-House Counsel did **not** approve the request. Rachel Dominguez's May 1, 2025 reply states verbatim: *"Before we can move forward on adding Nina Barros, though, let me review this with Sandra and get back to you. I also want to check where things stand against the current budget and staffing plan on our end. I'll try to circle back with you early next week."* No subsequent written approval appears anywhere in the production. The Firm's own Rate Schedule concedes the point, listing Barros's status as "Pending Approval — Added May 2025" with the note "Requested via email April 28, 2025; client response pending."

Notwithstanding the absence of approval, Barros billed 49.0 hours ($23,275.00) across fifteen entries from May 1 through May 30, performing interrogatory research, deposition-transcript review, and motion-related research — precisely the work the April 28 email said she would do. The June 1 Case Status Report asserts Barros's "involvement this month was consistent with the request submitted to Ms. Dominguez on April 28, 2025," but a *request* is not an *approval*. Section 4.2 is unambiguous: *"Staffing changes submitted after the fact, or where the new timekeeper has already commenced work prior to receiving written approval, are subject to reduction or rejection of all fees attributable to the unapproved timekeeper, at Pinnacle's sole discretion."*

The Firm also did not invoke the exigent-circumstances exception in Section 4.2. That exception requires notice "within twenty-four (24) hours" and a retroactive approval request explaining the exigency. The third document production and the May deposition schedule were foreseeable — the Firm knew of both when it wrote the April 28 email — and the Firm gave no 24-hour notice. The exception is therefore unavailable.

**Recommendation.** Write off all $23,275.00 attributable to Barros. If Pinnacle wishes to retain Barros prospectively, it should require a complete Section 4.2 staffing-change request (name, role, rate, reason, anticipated hours, and budget impact) and issue written approval before she bills further.

### Finding 2 — Unapproved Timekeeper: Priya Sengupta (Section 4.2)

**Reduction: $10,125.00 (full write-off of 27.0 hours × $375).**

Priya Sengupta, a first-year junior associate, is not on the Approved Staffing Plan. The Plan authorizes a single junior-associate slot filled from the Firm's rotating pool, then occupied by Tyler Webb, and states verbatim: *"only one junior associate will be assigned to and authorized to bill on this matter at any given time."* The Plan further provides that "any proposal to assign more than one junior associate concurrently would constitute a staffing change requiring prior written approval under Section 4.2."

Sengupta's addition as a **second** concurrent junior associate is therefore a staffing change requiring pre-approval. Unlike Barros, Sengupta was not even the subject of a staffing request: the April 28 email requested only Barros, and no separate request for Sengupta appears in the record. The Firm's Rate Schedule lists her status only as "Added May 2025," with no approval notation. She billed 27.0 hours ($10,125.00) across eleven entries from May 9 through May 30, performing DTSA research, exhibit-binder preparation, and cite-checking.

Section 4.2 again controls: fees attributable to an unapproved timekeeper are subject to reduction or rejection at Pinnacle's sole discretion, and no exigent-circumstance notice was given. In addition, to the extent Sengupta's early entries (e.g., Entry 31, May 9: "Participate in litigation team strategy discussion; begin research…") reflect onboarding, Section 6.3 renders "getting up to speed" time for a new timekeeper non-billable except for a single two-hour briefing — a further reason these fees are not recoverable even if she were approved.

**Recommendation.** Write off all $10,125.00 attributable to Sengupta. The Firm should not have assigned a second junior associate without approval; if it believed a second junior was needed, it was required to request one under Section 4.2 before she began work.

### Finding 3 — Contract Attorney Rate Exceeds Both the Approved Rate and the Cap (Sections 5.1 and 4.4)

**Reduction: $3,862.50 (154.5 hours × $25/hr).**

Marcus Tate, the contract attorney sourced through Redwood Document Solutions (an approved vendor), was billed at **$210/hr**. This rate violates two independent limits:

- **The Approved Staffing Plan rate of $185/hr.** Section 6 of the Plan ("Rate Schedule Confirmation") fixes the contract-attorney rate at "$185/hr (pass-through at vendor rates with no firm markup, consistent with the terms of the Firm's engagement by Pinnacle)." Section 4.4 of the Billing Guidelines requires that "[c]ontract attorney rates are subject to the maximum rate caps set forth in Section 5.1" and that contract-attorney costs be "passed through at actual cost with no markup by Outside Counsel." Billing $210 against an approved $185 pass-through rate either reflects an unauthorized markup or a rate change never approved in writing.
- **The Section 5.1 cap of $200/hr** for "Contract Attorney / Staff Attorney." Section 5.1 provides that "[r]ates in excess of these caps will be automatically reduced to the applicable cap."

The correct rate is the approved pass-through rate of $185/hr. At 154.5 line-item hours, the overcharge is 154.5 × ($210 − $185) = **$3,862.50**. (Even a reduction only to the $200 cap would yield 154.5 × $10 = $1,545.00; reduction to the approved $185 rate is the proper figure because that is the rate Pinnacle actually approved.)

**Recommendation.** Reduce Tate's rate to $185/hr for all 154.5 hours. Pinnacle should also request the underlying Redwood invoice to confirm that $185 is the true vendor pass-through cost and that no markup has been applied, as required by Section 4.4.

### Finding 4 — Block Billing (Section 5.4)

**Reduction: $30,220.62 (25% of 53 block-billed entries among approved timekeepers).**

Section 5.4 prohibits time entries that "combine two or more discrete tasks into a single line item" and requires that "[i]f Outside Counsel submits block-billed entries, Pinnacle will apply an automatic twenty-five percent (25%) reduction to the total fees of each block-billed entry." The reduction is "in addition to, and not in lieu of, any other reductions." Block billing is pervasive in this invoice. Fifty-three entries among the approved timekeepers combine distinct, independently timeable activities (e.g., drafting plus research; review plus client conference; deposition attendance plus post-deposition summary) into single lines. Representative examples:

- Entry 2 (Pettersson, 3.5 hrs): *"Draft responses to MedCore's Second Set of Interrogatories (Nos. 12–18); research Georgia Trade Secrets Act preemption of common-law claims…"* — drafting combined with research.
- Entry 12 (Cho, 4.5 hrs): *"Final review and revision of all interrogatory responses; prepare cover letter for service on opposing counsel; coordinate filing deadlines."* — three tasks.
- Entry 61 (Cho, 6.5 hrs): *"Prepare for and attend deposition of Dr. Rebecca Liu…; post-deposition analysis and team debrief."* — preparation, attendance, and debrief combined.
- Entry 92 (Hargrove, 6.0 hrs): *"Prepare for and conduct strategy session with Pinnacle in-house team…; draft outline for expert discovery plan. Review and edit interrogatory responses prepared by J. Pettersson."* — three tasks.
- Entry 9 (Hargrove, 4.5 hrs): *"Review and analyze documents produced by MedCore in third production batch; prepare detailed memorandum summarizing key findings for client."* — review combined with memorandum drafting.

The 25% reduction applies to each block-billed entry. The breakdown by timekeeper (approved timekeepers only; block-billed entries of the unapproved Barros and Sengupta are moot because those timekeepers are written off in full under Findings 1–2):

| Timekeeper | Block-Billed Entries | 25% Reduction |
|---|---|---|
| David Hargrove | 7 | $6,033.13 |
| Karen Cho | 21 | $13,515.63 |
| James Pettersson | 17 | $8,609.38 |
| Tyler Webb | 8 | $2,062.50 |
| Marcus Tate | 0 | $0.00 |
| **Total** | **53** | **$30,220.62** |

**Consequence and recommendation.** Apply the $30,220.62 reduction. Under Section 5.4, the Firm may avoid this reduction by resubmitting each block-billed entry "as separate line items with individually allocated time within fifteen (15) days of receiving notice of the reduction," provided the resubmitted entries "reasonably account for the total time originally reported." Pinnacle should offer that cure, but should scrutinize any resubmission to ensure the separated times are credible and do not inflate the total. Several block-billed entries also bundle non-compensable administrative sub-tasks (e.g., "coordinate filing deadlines," "coordinate with court clerk," "coordinate deposition logistics," "coordinate scheduling"); on resubmission, those administrative portions should be removed entirely under Section 6.1 rather than merely re-timed.

### Finding 5 — Administrative Task Billed at Attorney Rate (Section 6.1)

**Reduction: $712.50 (full write-off of Entry 15).**

Entry 15 (Pettersson, 1.5 hrs, $712.50): *"Schedule depositions of three remaining witnesses; coordinate court reporter availability; confirm deposition locations."* Each component is an Administrative Task under Section 2 and is presumptively non-compensable for any attorney under Section 6.1(g)–(k) (scheduling depositions; coordinating with court reporters; confirming locations). Section 6.1 provides that "[a]dministrative tasks by any attorney [are] written off in full, or reduced to the paralegal rate at Pinnacle's discretion." These are core overhead functions; Grayson Court Reporting Services is the approved court-reporting vendor and scheduling with it is logistical, not legal.

**Recommendation.** Write off the full $712.50. (Pinnacle may, in its discretion, allow 1.5 hours at the $175 paralegal rate = $262.50 instead, a $450.00 reduction; full write-off is recommended because the entire entry is administrative.)

### Finding 6 — Task-to-Timekeeper Appropriateness (Section 6.1)

**Reduction: $3,385.00 across three entries.**

Section 6.1 requires that "[e]ach task [be assigned] to the lowest-cost timekeeper capable of competently performing the work" and prescribes rate reductions where a senior timekeeper performs work appropriate to a lower level.

**(a) Cite-checking by a partner — Entry 43 — $1,220.00.** Entry 43 (Hargrove, 2.0 hrs, $1,970.00): *"Review and revise cite-check memorandum prepared by T. Webb; finalize citations for motion to compel brief."* This is cite-checking. Section 6.1(b) identifies cite-checking as appropriate for "junior associate or paralegal," and the prescribed reduction is that "[c]ite-checking by a partner or senior associate [is] reduced to the junior associate rate." Reducing 2.0 hours from $985 to the $375 junior-associate rate yields 2.0 × $375 = $750.00, a reduction of **$1,220.00**. (Tyler Webb, the approved junior associate, was already performing cite-checking on this same brief — Entry 47 — making the partner's duplicate cite-checking also an over-lawyering concern under Section 6.2.)

**(b) Routine document review by a junior associate — Entry 79 — $665.00.** Entry 79 (Webb, 3.5 hrs, $1,312.50): *"Review third batch of MedCore production documents for relevance and privilege."* This is routine first-level document review for relevance and privilege — the core function the Approved Staffing Plan assigns to the contract attorney (Marcus Tate), who was concurrently reviewing the same production with detailed Bates-range narratives. Section 6.1 provides that "[d]ocument review by a junior associate that constitutes routine first-level review for relevance or responsiveness [is] reduced to the contract attorney rate." Reducing 3.5 hours from $375 to the $185 contract-attorney rate yields 3.5 × $185 = $647.50, a reduction of **$665.00**. The narrative is also generic ("Review… documents for relevance and privilege") and borders on the unacceptable descriptions identified in Section 9.2 (compare Tate's entries, which specify Bates ranges); the Firm should resubmit with an adequate narrative if it seeks payment for any substantive review.

**(c) Deposition exhibit-binder preparation by a mid-level associate — Entry 56 — $1,500.00.** Entry 56 (Pettersson, 5.0 hrs, $2,375.00): *"Prepare deposition exhibits and exhibit list for Dr. Rebecca Liu deposition; organize documents by topic area; prepare witness examination binder."* Preparation of deposition exhibit binders is identified in Section 6.1(c) as appropriate for "paralegal or junior associate." Reducing 5.0 hours from $475 to the $175 paralegal rate yields 5.0 × $175 = $875.00, a reduction of **$1,500.00**. (The unapproved Sengupta also performed exhibit-binder work — Entries 42, 59, 97 — but those fees are already written off under Finding 2.)

**Recommendation.** Apply the $3,385.00 in rate reductions. On resubmission, the Firm should re-time these entries at the appropriate level or assign the work to the appropriate timekeeper going forward.

### Finding 7 — Conference and Deposition Staffing in Excess of Section 4.3 Limits

**Reduction: $3,312.50.**

Section 4.3 limits billing timekeepers for internal meetings and depositions. For internal meetings and strategy sessions, "the total number of billing timekeepers for any single internal meeting or conference call shall not exceed three (3)." For depositions, "Outside Counsel shall seek advance approval from the Designated In-House Counsel if it believes a particular deposition warrants more than two (2) billing timekeepers," with requests due "no later than five (5) business days before the scheduled deposition"; "[i]n the absence of such advance approval, Pinnacle reserves the right to reduce the time of the additional timekeepers in their entirety."

**(a) May 8 team strategy call — five billing timekeepers — $937.50.** The June 1 Case Status Report states that on May 8 "the full litigation team participated in a strategy conference call." The invoice shows five timekeepers billing that call: Hargrove (Entry 22, 3.0 hrs), Cho (Entry 23, 3.0 hrs), Pettersson (Entry 24, 3.0 hrs), Barros (Entry 25, 2.5 hrs), and Webb (Entry 26, 2.5 hrs). Even assuming this was a Substantive Case-Strategy Session, the three-timekeeper cap applies. Five billers exceed the cap by two. Barros is already written off in full (Finding 1); of the remaining four approved billers, one is excess. No advance designation of billing timekeepers was made, so Pinnacle elects to retain the three core strategists (Hargrove, Cho, Pettersson) and reduce the most junior excess biller, Webb. Entry 26 (2.5 hrs, $937.50) is reduced in its entirety. **Reduction: $937.50.**

**(b) Dr. Rebecca Liu deposition (May 19) — three billing timekeepers — $2,375.00.** The Case Status Report states that Hargrove, Cho, and Pettersson attended the Liu deposition and that "[a]ll three attorneys were present for the full duration of the deposition." Three billing timekeepers for a deposition exceeds the two-timekeeper default in Section 4.3. No advance approval was requested (it would have been due no later than May 12, five business days before May 19), and none appears in the record. Pinnacle retains the two examining/strategic attorneys — Cho (who conducted the direct examination, Entry 61) and Hargrove (who attended to assess credibility and guide follow-up, Entry 60) — and reduces the additional timekeeper, Pettersson, in his entirety. Pettersson's role (exhibit management, note-taking, and testimony summary) is support work that Section 6.1 indicates is appropriate for a paralegal or junior associate in any event. Entry 62 (5.0 hrs, $2,375.00) is reduced in its entirety. **Reduction: $2,375.00.** (If the Firm contends the post-deposition summary portion was performed separately from the deposition, it may resubmit that portion as a separate, adequately described entry at the appropriate timekeeper level.)

**Recommendation.** Apply the $3,312.50 reduction. Going forward, the Firm must designate billing timekeepers in advance for any meeting expected to exceed three billers and must request additional deposition staffing at least five business days ahead, as Section 4.3 requires.

### Finding 8 — Monthly Budget Overage Without Pre-Approval (Section 7.3)

**No additional dollar reduction (subsumed by the reductions above); violation noted.**

The Approved Staffing Plan sets a monthly fee range of $95,000.00 to $165,000.00 and, per Section 7.3, a maximum payable without pre-approval of 115% of the high end, i.e., $165,000 × 1.15 = **$189,750.00**. Section 7.3 requires the Firm to notify the Designated In-House Counsel and obtain written pre-approval before monthly fees exceed that threshold.

Both the invoice's stated fee total ($193,372.50) and the line-item total ($215,680.00) exceed the $189,750.00 threshold — by $3,622.50 and $25,930.00 respectively. No pre-approval was sought or given; the April 28 / May 1 correspondence addressed only the Barros staffing request, not a budget overage, and the June 1 Case Status Report contains no Section 7.3 overage notification. The Firm therefore violated the Section 7.3 notice and pre-approval requirement.

Section 7.3 permits Pinnacle to "reduce the invoice to the approved budget ceiling plus fifteen percent (15%)" — i.e., to $189,750.00 — and provides that this remedy "does not limit Pinnacle's right to apply other reductions." Because the substantive reductions in Findings 1–7 already bring the payable fee amount to $140,786.88, well below $189,750.00, the budget cap does not operate as an *additional* dollar reduction here. The violation is nonetheless recorded: the Firm exceeded the approved range and the 115% threshold without the required pre-approval, and Pinnacle retains the right to treat any future unapproved overage under Section 7.3.

**Recommendation.** No additional reduction is required on this invoice, but Pinnacle should (i) notify the Firm in writing that the Section 7.3 pre-approval requirement was not satisfied, and (ii) require a Section 7.3 overage notification (projected total, reason, driving tasks/timekeepers, and revised estimate) before approving any future month that may exceed $189,750.00.

### Finding 9 — Expense Review (Sections 8.1–8.4, 6.4)

**Reduction: $2,100.00 (travel); $3,700.00 flagged for verification.**

The invoice claims $8,450.00 in expenses across four items.

**(a) Travel — $2,100.00 — non-reimbursable; full write-off.** Expense 2 (May 18, "Various," $2,100.00): *"Travel expenses for D. Hargrove and K. Cho to attend Dr. Rebecca Liu deposition — mileage, parking, meals."* The Case Status Report states that the Liu deposition "was taken at the offices of Hargrove & Linden LLP in Atlanta, Georgia." Hargrove and Cho therefore traveled to their **own firm's office** in Atlanta to attend a deposition held there. Section 6.4 provides that "[l]ocal travel within the Atlanta metropolitan area is not billable under any circumstances." Section 8.2 further renders meals non-reimbursable except during approved out-of-town travel, which did not occur. Separately, Section 8.3 requires pre-approval for any travel exceeding $1,000 in the aggregate, and no such approval appears in the record. The entire $2,100.00 is non-reimbursable. **Reduction: $2,100.00.**

**(b) Court reporting — $3,200.00 — approved vendor; verify expedited premium.** Expense 1 (Grayson Court Reporting Services, $3,200.00) covers the court reporter and transcript for the Liu deposition, "original transcript plus one copy, expedited delivery." Grayson is an approved vendor (Appendix B), and Section 8.1 reimburses "court reporter fees" and "deposition transcript costs (one copy per deposition, at standard rates)." The "expedited delivery" descriptor suggests a premium over standard rates. Pinnacle should require the underlying Grayson invoice and reduce any expedited surcharge that exceeds standard transcript rates. **No automatic reduction; flag for verification.**

**(c) Electronic discovery hosting — $2,650.00 — approved vendor; require vendor invoice.** Expense 3 (Tripoint Litigation Support LLC, $2,650.00) is monthly hosting for the ~85,000-document third production. Tripoint is the approved e-discovery vendor identified in the Approved Staffing Plan, and the amount is reasonable for that volume. Section 8.4 requires vendor costs to be "passed through at actual cost with no markup" and documented with the vendor invoice. **No reduction; require the Tripoint invoice to confirm pass-through at actual cost.**

**(d) Courier / delivery — $500.00 — verify time-sensitive justification and receipts.** Expense 4 ("Various," $500.00) covers "service of discovery documents and filing of motion to compel." Section 8.1 reimburses "messenger and courier services when time-sensitive delivery is required." Two concerns: (i) the motion to compel would have been filed electronically, which does not require a courier; and (ii) service on opposing counsel (Sternfield Morrison & Katz LLP, also in Atlanta) could ordinarily be accomplished by electronic or standard means. Pinnacle should require receipts and a time-sensitive justification; absent that, the $500.00 should be reduced. **No automatic reduction; flag for verification.**

**Recommendation.** Write off $2,100.00 (travel). Hold $3,700.00 (court reporting $3,200.00 + courier $500.00) pending vendor invoices and time-sensitive justification; pay the $2,650.00 Tripoint charge upon receipt of the vendor invoice confirming pass-through at actual cost. Recommended payable expenses: $6,350.00, of which $3,700.00 remains conditional on documentation.

### Finding 10 — Over-Lawyering / Redundant Senior Review (Section 6.2) — Observation

Section 6.2 prohibits "over-lawyering" — "multiple levels of attorney review of the same work product beyond what is reasonably necessary for quality control" — and provides that "[a] single round of review by the responsible supervising attorney is presumptively reasonable; additional rounds of review or involvement of additional senior timekeepers require justification." The invoice reflects a recurring pattern of three-level review that exceeds the presumptively reasonable single supervisory round:

- **Interrogatory responses:** drafted by Pettersson (Entries 2, 6, 11), reviewed and revised by Cho (Entries 3, 7, 12), and then re-edited by Hargrove on May 27 (Entry 92: "Review and edit interrogatory responses prepared by J. Pettersson"). That is a third review level, performed by the lead partner, with no stated justification.
- **Motion to compel:** drafted by Pettersson (Entries 19, 29), reviewed by Cho (Entries 20, 30, 35), and reviewed again by Hargrove (Entries 32, 43). Hargrove's Entry 43 cite-checking (Finding 6(a)) is particularly difficult to justify given that the approved junior associate was already cite-checking the same brief (Entry 47).
- **Deposition outlines (Liu and Krenshaw):** prepared by Pettersson, reviewed by Cho, and then conferenced with Hargrove, repeatedly.

No separate dollar reduction is assigned here because the redundant senior review is captured within the block-billed entries (Finding 4) and, in the case of Entry 43, the task-appropriateness reduction (Finding 6(a)). **Recommendation.** On resubmission, the Firm should either justify or remove the redundant senior review time. Going forward, Pinnacle should enforce the single-round-of-supervisory-review presumption and require justification for any additional senior review.

### Finding 11 — Insufficient Time Narrative (Section 9.2) — Observation

Section 9.2 requires narratives "sufficiently detailed to allow Pinnacle to assess the nature, necessity, and reasonableness of the work performed" and rejects generic descriptions such as "review documents." Most entries in this invoice are adequately described (Tate's document-review entries, for example, specify Bates ranges). The principal exception is Entry 79 (Webb): *"Review third batch of MedCore production documents for relevance and privilege"* — a generic description that does not identify the documents reviewed or the issues addressed, and that is indistinguishable from the routine first-level review the contract attorney was performing concurrently. This entry is reduced under Finding 6(b) for task appropriateness; if the Firm resubmits it, the narrative must be brought into Section 9.2 compliance. **Recommendation.** Reject any resubmitted entry that does not meet Section 9.2's specificity standard.

### Finding 12 — Billing Increments (Section 5.5) — Observation (Compliant)

Section 5.5 requires time in increments "of no greater than one-tenth (0.1) of an hour" and rejects quarter-hour (0.25) increments. All 117 entries are recorded in 0.5-hour multiples, which are valid 0.1-increment multiples; no quarter-hour (0.25) entries were found, so no Section 5.5 rounding reduction applies. Pinnacle should note, however, that the uniform 0.5-hour granularity is coarse and may overstate time on short tasks; the Firm should record time in true 0.1-hour increments going forward. **No reduction.**

### Finding 13 — Partner Hours Exceed the Approved Monthly Range — Observation

The Approved Staffing Plan projects 15–25 hours/month for David Hargrove. He billed 38.5 hours in May — 13.5 hours (54%) above the high end of the approved range. The Case Status Report acknowledges that "[m]r. Hargrove's involvement was significantly more extensive than in prior months" and predicts a return to typical levels. Exceeding an individual hour range is not, by itself, an automatic reduction under the Billing Guidelines (budget impact is governed by Section 7.3, addressed in Finding 8). The overage is nonetheless a flag: it helped drive the invoice over the Section 7.3 threshold, and a portion of the partner time was spent on tasks more appropriately delegated (document review, Entry 9; cite-checking, Entry 43). **No separate reduction; noted for budget-management follow-up.**

### Finding 14 — Rate-Cap Compliance for Approved Timekeepers — Observation (Compliant)

Apart from Marcus Tate (Finding 3), the approved timekeepers' rates are within the Section 5.1 caps: Hargrove $985 (cap $1,000); Cho $625 (cap $650); Pettersson $475 (cap $500); Webb $375 (cap $400). No reclassification of timekeepers (e.g., a contract attorney billed as a junior associate) was detected. **No reduction.**

## I. Executive Summary

This memorandum reviews Invoice No. HL-2025-05-PIN, submitted by Hargrove & Linden LLP (the "Firm") on June 5, 2025 for legal services rendered during May 1–31, 2025 in the captioned matter. The review applies the Firm's Approved Discovery-Phase Staffing Plan (dated February 10, 2025), Pinnacle's Outside Counsel Billing Guidelines (effective January 1, 2023) (the "Billing Guidelines"), the April 28 / May 1, 2025 staffing correspondence, and the Firm's June 1, 2025 Monthly Case Status Report.

The invoice should not be paid as submitted. The review identifies **fourteen distinct issues**, the most serious of which are:

1. **The invoice does not reconcile internally.** The Time Detail line items actually total **$215,680.00 in attorney fees (483.5 hours)**, but the invoice's Summary page and the Time Detail "SUBTOTAL" row both state **$193,372.50 (422.5 hours)** — an unexplained discrepancy of **$22,307.50 and 61.0 hours**. The invoice therefore fails the Section 9.1 requirement that the summary page and time detail page reconcile, and it cannot be processed until the Firm explains the variance.

2. **Two timekeepers who billed the entire month were never approved.** Nina Barros (mid-level associate, 49.0 hours, $23,275.00) and Priya Sengupta (junior associate, 27.0 hours, $10,125.00) are not on the Approved Staffing Plan. The Firm's own Rate Schedule admits Barros is "Pending Approval" and Sengupta was simply "Added May 2025." The April 28 staffing email requested only Barros, and Pinnacle's May 1 reply expressly deferred approval ("let me review this with Sandra and get back to you"); no approval ever issued. Under Section 4.2, all fees attributable to unapproved timekeepers are subject to reduction or rejection — a combined **$33,400.00**.

3. **The contract attorney was billed above both the approved rate and the rate cap.** Marcus Tate was billed at $210/hr, exceeding both the Approved Staffing Plan rate of $185/hr (pass-through, no markup) and the Section 5.1 cap of $200/hr — a **$3,862.50** overcharge across 154.5 hours.

4. **Block billing is pervasive.** Fifty-three (53) entries among the approved timekeepers combine two or more discrete tasks into a single line, triggering the automatic 25% reduction under Section 5.4 — **$30,220.62**.

5. **A single internal strategy call was billed by five timekeepers and a key deposition by three, both in excess of the Section 4.3 limits** and without the required advance approval — **$3,312.50**.

6. **Travel expense of $2,100.00 is non-reimbursable.** It was incurred for two attorneys to travel to the Firm's own Atlanta office for a deposition taken there; local travel within the Atlanta metropolitan area is not billable under any circumstances (Section 6.4).

After applying all reductions to the line-item fee base of $215,680.00, the recommended payable attorney fees are **$140,786.88**. After expense review, recommended payable expenses are **$6,350.00** (with an additional $3,700.00 flagged for verification). The **recommended total payable is $147,136.88**, a reduction of **$54,685.62** from the $201,822.50 requested.

The invoice should be returned to the Firm with this memorandum's itemized reductions, with payment of $147,136.88 (at most) held pending (a) reconciliation of the $22,307.50 internal discrepancy, (b) documentation for the $3,700.00 of flagged expenses, and (c) the Firm's election to resubmit block-billed and task-misassigned entries with separated time under the cure provisions of Sections 5.4 and 9.2.

## II. Documents Reviewed

## III. Threshold Issue — The Invoice Does Not Reconcile Internally (Section 9.1)

Before reaching the substantive reductions, a threshold defect must be flagged. Section 9.1 requires each invoice to include both "a summary page showing total fees by timekeeper (name, role, hours, rate, and amount)" and "a time detail page with individual entries." The two must reconcile. They do not.

The Summary page and the Time Detail "SUBTOTAL" row each state attorney fees of **$193,372.50 (422.5 hours)**. However, the individual line items in the Time Detail actually sum to **$215,680.00 (483.5 hours)** — a discrepancy of **$22,307.50 and 61.0 hours** that the invoice does not explain. The variance is not concentrated in one timekeeper; it appears across most of the team:

| Tyler Webb | 35.0 / $13,125.00 | 34.0 / $12,750.00 | −1.0 / −$375.00 |

| **Total** | **422.5 / $193,372.50** | **483.5 / $215,680.00** | **+61.0 / +$22,307.50** |

**Consequence and recommendation.** Because the line items exceed the stated total, the invoice as presented is internally inconsistent and cannot be paid on its face. The substantive reductions in this memorandum are computed against the **line-item** base of $215,680.00, because the line items are the granular record of the work actually claimed and are where every substantive defect (block billing, task misassignment, etc.) resides. Pinnacle should require the Firm to reconcile the $22,307.50 / 61.0-hour variance before any payment, and should not pay any amount attributable to line items the Firm cannot substantiate. The recommended payable figure below assumes the line-item base is the correct one; if the Firm instead contends the $193,372.50 figure is correct, it must identify and withdraw the excess 61.0 hours of line items.

---

## IV. Findings and Reduction Calculations

### Finding 1 — Unapproved Timekeeper: Nina Barros (Section 4.2)

**Reduction: $23,275.00 (full write-off of 49.0 hours × $475).**

Nina Barros, a fourth-year mid-level associate, is not on the Approved Staffing Plan. The Plan is explicit that "James Pettersson is the only mid-level associate assigned to this matter. No other mid-level associate is included on the approved staffing roster," and that any addition requires "prior written approval" under Section 4.2.

The record shows the Firm attempted to obtain approval but did not succeed. On April 28, 2025, Karen Cho emailed Rachel Dominguez requesting Barros's addition at "$475/hr" for an estimated "40–50 hours per month." Pinnacle's Designated In-House Counsel did **not** approve the request. Rachel Dominguez's May 1, 2025 reply states verbatim: *"Before we can move forward on adding Nina Barros, though, let me review this with Sandra and get back to you. I also want to check where things stand against the current budget and staffing plan on our end. I'll try to circle back with you early next week."* No subsequent written approval appears anywhere in the production. The Firm's own Rate Schedule concedes the point, listing Barros's status as "Pending Approval — Added May 2025" with the note "Requested via email April 28, 2025; client response pending."

Notwithstanding the absence of approval, Barros billed 49.0 hours ($23,275.00) across fifteen entries from May 1 through May 30, performing interrogatory research, deposition-transcript review, and motion-related research — precisely the work the April 28 email said she would do. The June 1 Case Status Report asserts Barros's "involvement this month was consistent with the request submitted to Ms. Dominguez on April 28, 2025," but a *request* is not an *approval*. Section 4.2 is unambiguous: *"Staffing changes submitted after the fact, or where the new timekeeper has already commenced work prior to receiving written approval, are subject to reduction or rejection of all fees attributable to the unapproved timekeeper, at Pinnacle's sole discretion."*

The Firm also did not invoke the exigent-circumstances exception in Section 4.2. That exception requires notice "within twenty-four (24) hours" and a retroactive approval request explaining the exigency. The third document production and the May deposition schedule were foreseeable — the Firm knew of both when it wrote the April 28 email — and the Firm gave no 24-hour notice. The exception is therefore unavailable.

**Recommendation.** Write off all $23,275.00 attributable to Barros. If Pinnacle wishes to retain Barros prospectively, it should require a complete Section 4.2 staffing-change request (name, role, rate, reason, anticipated hours, and budget impact) and issue written approval before she bills further.

### Finding 2 — Unapproved Timekeeper: Priya Sengupta (Section 4.2)

**Reduction: $10,125.00 (full write-off of 27.0 hours × $375).**

Priya Sengupta, a first-year junior associate, is not on the Approved Staffing Plan. The Plan authorizes a single junior-associate slot filled from the Firm's rotating pool, then occupied by Tyler Webb, and states verbatim: *"only one junior associate will be assigned to and authorized to bill on this matter at any given time."* The Plan further provides that "any proposal to assign more than one junior associate concurrently would constitute a staffing change requiring prior written approval under Section 4.2."

Sengupta's addition as a **second** concurrent junior associate is therefore a staffing change requiring pre-approval. Unlike Barros, Sengupta was not even the subject of a staffing request: the April 28 email requested only Barros, and no separate request for Sengupta appears in the record. The Firm's Rate Schedule lists her status only as "Added May 2025," with no approval notation. She billed 27.0 hours ($10,125.00) across eleven entries from May 9 through May 30, performing DTSA research, exhibit-binder preparation, and cite-checking.

Section 4.2 again controls: fees attributable to an unapproved timekeeper are subject to reduction or rejection at Pinnacle's sole discretion, and no exigent-circumstance notice was given. In addition, to the extent Sengupta's early entries (e.g., Entry 31, May 9: "Participate in litigation team strategy discussion; begin research…") reflect onboarding, Section 6.3 renders "getting up to speed" time for a new timekeeper non-billable except for a single two-hour briefing — a further reason these fees are not recoverable even if she were approved.

**Recommendation.** Write off all $10,125.00 attributable to Sengupta. The Firm should not have assigned a second junior associate without approval; if it believed a second junior was needed, it was required to request one under Section 4.2 before she began work.

### Finding 3 — Contract Attorney Rate Exceeds Both the Approved Rate and the Cap (Sections 5.1 and 4.4)

**Reduction: $3,862.50 (154.5 hours × $25/hr).**

Marcus Tate, the contract attorney sourced through Redwood Document Solutions (an approved vendor), was billed at **$210/hr**. This rate violates two independent limits:

- **The Approved Staffing Plan rate of $185/hr.** Section 6 of the Plan ("Rate Schedule Confirmation") fixes the contract-attorney rate at "$185/hr (pass-through at vendor rates with no firm markup, consistent with the terms of the Firm's engagement by Pinnacle)." Section 4.4 of the Billing Guidelines requires that "[c]ontract attorney rates are subject to the maximum rate caps set forth in Section 5.1" and that contract-attorney costs be "passed through at actual cost with no markup by Outside Counsel." Billing $210 against an approved $185 pass-through rate either reflects an unauthorized markup or a rate change never approved in writing.
- **The Section 5.1 cap of $200/hr** for "Contract Attorney / Staff Attorney." Section 5.1 provides that "[r]ates in excess of these caps will be automatically reduced to the applicable cap."

The correct rate is the approved pass-through rate of $185/hr. At 154.5 line-item hours, the overcharge is 154.5 × ($210 − $185) = **$3,862.50**. (Even a reduction only to the $200 cap would yield 154.5 × $10 = $1,545.00; reduction to the approved $185 rate is the proper figure because that is the rate Pinnacle actually approved.)

**Recommendation.** Reduce Tate's rate to $185/hr for all 154.5 hours. Pinnacle should also request the underlying Redwood invoice to confirm that $185 is the true vendor pass-through cost and that no markup has been applied, as required by Section 4.4.

### Finding 4 — Block Billing (Section 5.4)

**Reduction: $30,220.62 (25% of 53 block-billed entries among approved timekeepers).**

Section 5.4 prohibits time entries that "combine two or more discrete tasks into a single line item" and requires that "[i]f Outside Counsel submits block-billed entries, Pinnacle will apply an automatic twenty-five percent (25%) reduction to the total fees of each block-billed entry." The reduction is "in addition to, and not in lieu of, any other reductions." Block billing is pervasive in this invoice. Fifty-three entries among the approved timekeepers combine distinct, independently timeable activities (e.g., drafting plus research; review plus client conference; deposition attendance plus post-deposition summary) into single lines. Representative examples:

- Entry 2 (Pettersson, 3.5 hrs): *"Draft responses to MedCore's Second Set of Interrogatories (Nos. 12–18); research Georgia Trade Secrets Act preemption of common-law claims…"* — drafting combined with research.
- Entry 12 (Cho, 4.5 hrs): *"Final review and revision of all interrogatory responses; prepare cover letter for service on opposing counsel; coordinate filing deadlines."* — three tasks.
- Entry 61 (Cho, 6.5 hrs): *"Prepare for and attend deposition of Dr. Rebecca Liu…; post-deposition analysis and team debrief."* — preparation, attendance, and debrief combined.
- Entry 92 (Hargrove, 6.0 hrs): *"Prepare for and conduct strategy session with Pinnacle in-house team…; draft outline for expert discovery plan. Review and edit interrogatory responses prepared by J. Pettersson."* — three tasks.
- Entry 9 (Hargrove, 4.5 hrs): *"Review and analyze documents produced by MedCore in third production batch; prepare detailed memorandum summarizing key findings for client."* — review combined with memorandum drafting.

The 25% reduction applies to each block-billed entry. The breakdown by timekeeper (approved timekeepers only; block-billed entries of the unapproved Barros and Sengupta are moot because those timekeepers are written off in full under Findings 1–2):

| Timekeeper | Block-Billed Entries | 25% Reduction |
|---|---|---|
| David Hargrove | 7 | $6,033.13 |
| Karen Cho | 21 | $13,515.63 |
| James Pettersson | 17 | $8,609.38 |
| Tyler Webb | 8 | $2,062.50 |
| Marcus Tate | 0 | $0.00 |
| **Total** | **53** | **$30,220.62** |

**Consequence and recommendation.** Apply the $30,220.62 reduction. Under Section 5.4, the Firm may avoid this reduction by resubmitting each block-billed entry "as separate line items with individually allocated time within fifteen (15) days of receiving notice of the reduction," provided the resubmitted entries "reasonably account for the total time originally reported." Pinnacle should offer that cure, but should scrutinize any resubmission to ensure the separated times are credible and do not inflate the total. Several block-billed entries also bundle non-compensable administrative sub-tasks (e.g., "coordinate filing deadlines," "coordinate with court clerk," "coordinate deposition logistics," "coordinate scheduling"); on resubmission, those administrative portions should be removed entirely under Section 6.1 rather than merely re-timed.

### Finding 5 — Administrative Task Billed at Attorney Rate (Section 6.1)

**Reduction: $712.50 (full write-off of Entry 15).**

Entry 15 (Pettersson, 1.5 hrs, $712.50): *"Schedule depositions of three remaining witnesses; coordinate court reporter availability; confirm deposition locations."* Each component is an Administrative Task under Section 2 and is presumptively non-compensable for any attorney under Section 6.1(g)–(k) (scheduling depositions; coordinating with court reporters; confirming locations). Section 6.1 provides that "[a]dministrative tasks by any attorney [are] written off in full, or reduced to the paralegal rate at Pinnacle's discretion." These are core overhead functions; Grayson Court Reporting Services is the approved court-reporting vendor and scheduling with it is logistical, not legal.

**Recommendation.** Write off the full $712.50. (Pinnacle may, in its discretion, allow 1.5 hours at the $175 paralegal rate = $262.50 instead, a $450.00 reduction; full write-off is recommended because the entire entry is administrative.)

### Finding 6 — Task-to-Timekeeper Appropriateness (Section 6.1)

**Reduction: $3,385.00 across three entries.**

Section 6.1 requires that "[e]ach task [be assigned] to the lowest-cost timekeeper capable of competently performing the work" and prescribes rate reductions where a senior timekeeper performs work appropriate to a lower level.

**(a) Cite-checking by a partner — Entry 43 — $1,220.00.** Entry 43 (Hargrove, 2.0 hrs, $1,970.00): *"Review and revise cite-check memorandum prepared by T. Webb; finalize citations for motion to compel brief."* This is cite-checking. Section 6.1(b) identifies cite-checking as appropriate for "junior associate or paralegal," and the prescribed reduction is that "[c]ite-checking by a partner or senior associate [is] reduced to the junior associate rate." Reducing 2.0 hours from $985 to the $375 junior-associate rate yields 2.0 × $375 = $750.00, a reduction of **$1,220.00**. (Tyler Webb, the approved junior associate, was already performing cite-checking on this same brief — Entry 47 — making the partner's duplicate cite-checking also an over-lawyering concern under Section 6.2.)

**(b) Routine document review by a junior associate — Entry 79 — $665.00.** Entry 79 (Webb, 3.5 hrs, $1,312.50): *"Review third batch of MedCore production documents for relevance and privilege."* This is routine first-level document review for relevance and privilege — the core function the Approved Staffing Plan assigns to the contract attorney (Marcus Tate), who was concurrently reviewing the same production with detailed Bates-range narratives. Section 6.1 provides that "[d]ocument review by a junior associate that constitutes routine first-level review for relevance or responsiveness [is] reduced to the contract attorney rate." Reducing 3.5 hours from $375 to the $185 contract-attorney rate yields 3.5 × $185 = $647.50, a reduction of **$665.00**. The narrative is also generic ("Review… documents for relevance and privilege") and borders on the unacceptable descriptions identified in Section 9.2 (compare Tate's entries, which specify Bates ranges); the Firm should resubmit with an adequate narrative if it seeks payment for any substantive review.

**(c) Deposition exhibit-binder preparation by a mid-level associate — Entry 56 — $1,500.00.** Entry 56 (Pettersson, 5.0 hrs, $2,375.00): *"Prepare deposition exhibits and exhibit list for Dr. Rebecca Liu deposition; organize documents by topic area; prepare witness examination binder."* Preparation of deposition exhibit binders is identified in Section 6.1(c) as appropriate for "paralegal or junior associate." Reducing 5.0 hours from $475 to the $175 paralegal rate yields 5.0 × $175 = $875.00, a reduction of **$1,500.00**. (The unapproved Sengupta also performed exhibit-binder work — Entries 42, 59, 97 — but those fees are already written off under Finding 2.)

**Recommendation.** Apply the $3,385.00 in rate reductions. On resubmission, the Firm should re-time these entries at the appropriate level or assign the work to the appropriate timekeeper going forward.

### Finding 7 — Conference and Deposition Staffing in Excess of Section 4.3 Limits

**Reduction: $3,312.50.**

Section 4.3 limits billing timekeepers for internal meetings and depositions. For internal meetings and strategy sessions, "the total number of billing timekeepers for any single internal meeting or conference call shall not exceed three (3)." For depositions, "Outside Counsel shall seek advance approval from the Designated In-House Counsel if it believes a particular deposition warrants more than two (2) billing timekeepers," with requests due "no later than five (5) business days before the scheduled deposition"; "[i]n the absence of such advance approval, Pinnacle reserves the right to reduce the time of the additional timekeepers in their entirety."

**(a) May 8 team strategy call — five billing timekeepers — $937.50.** The June 1 Case Status Report states that on May 8 "the full litigation team participated in a strategy conference call." The invoice shows five timekeepers billing that call: Hargrove (Entry 22, 3.0 hrs), Cho (Entry 23, 3.0 hrs), Pettersson (Entry 24, 3.0 hrs), Barros (Entry 25, 2.5 hrs), and Webb (Entry 26, 2.5 hrs). Even assuming this was a Substantive Case-Strategy Session, the three-timekeeper cap applies. Five billers exceed the cap by two. Barros is already written off in full (Finding 1); of the remaining four approved billers, one is excess. No advance designation of billing timekeepers was made, so Pinnacle elects to retain the three core strategists (Hargrove, Cho, Pettersson) and reduce the most junior excess biller, Webb. Entry 26 (2.5 hrs, $937.50) is reduced in its entirety. **Reduction: $937.50.**

**(b) Dr. Rebecca Liu deposition (May 19) — three billing timekeepers — $2,375.00.** The Case Status Report states that Hargrove, Cho, and Pettersson attended the Liu deposition and that "[a]ll three attorneys were present for the full duration of the deposition." Three billing timekeepers for a deposition exceeds the two-timekeeper default in Section 4.3. No advance approval was requested (it would have been due no later than May 12, five business days before May 19), and none appears in the record. Pinnacle retains the two examining/strategic attorneys — Cho (who conducted the direct examination, Entry 61) and Hargrove (who attended to assess credibility and guide follow-up, Entry 60) — and reduces the additional timekeeper, Pettersson, in his entirety. Pettersson's role (exhibit management, note-taking, and testimony summary) is support work that Section 6.1 indicates is appropriate for a paralegal or junior associate in any event. Entry 62 (5.0 hrs, $2,375.00) is reduced in its entirety. **Reduction: $2,375.00.** (If the Firm contends the post-deposition summary portion was performed separately from the deposition, it may resubmit that portion as a separate, adequately described entry at the appropriate timekeeper level.)

**Recommendation.** Apply the $3,312.50 reduction. Going forward, the Firm must designate billing timekeepers in advance for any meeting expected to exceed three billers and must request additional deposition staffing at least five business days ahead, as Section 4.3 requires.

### Finding 8 — Monthly Budget Overage Without Pre-Approval (Section 7.3)

**No additional dollar reduction (subsumed by the reductions above); violation noted.**

The Approved Staffing Plan sets a monthly fee range of $95,000.00 to $165,000.00 and, per Section 7.3, a maximum payable without pre-approval of 115% of the high end, i.e., $165,000 × 1.15 = **$189,750.00**. Section 7.3 requires the Firm to notify the Designated In-House Counsel and obtain written pre-approval before monthly fees exceed that threshold.

Both the invoice's stated fee total ($193,372.50) and the line-item total ($215,680.00) exceed the $189,750.00 threshold — by $3,622.50 and $25,930.00 respectively. No pre-approval was sought or given; the April 28 / May 1 correspondence addressed only the Barros staffing request, not a budget overage, and the June 1 Case Status Report contains no Section 7.3 overage notification. The Firm therefore violated the Section 7.3 notice and pre-approval requirement.

Section 7.3 permits Pinnacle to "reduce the invoice to the approved budget ceiling plus fifteen percent (15%)" — i.e., to $189,750.00 — and provides that this remedy "does not limit Pinnacle's right to apply other reductions." Because the substantive reductions in Findings 1–7 already bring the payable fee amount to $140,786.88, well below $189,750.00, the budget cap does not operate as an *additional* dollar reduction here. The violation is nonetheless recorded: the Firm exceeded the approved range and the 115% threshold without the required pre-approval, and Pinnacle retains the right to treat any future unapproved overage under Section 7.3.

**Recommendation.** No additional reduction is required on this invoice, but Pinnacle should (i) notify the Firm in writing that the Section 7.3 pre-approval requirement was not satisfied, and (ii) require a Section 7.3 overage notification (projected total, reason, driving tasks/timekeepers, and revised estimate) before approving any future month that may exceed $189,750.00.

### Finding 9 — Expense Review (Sections 8.1–8.4, 6.4)

**Reduction: $2,100.00 (travel); $3,700.00 flagged for verification.**

The invoice claims $8,450.00 in expenses across four items.

**(a) Travel — $2,100.00 — non-reimbursable; full write-off.** Expense 2 (May 18, "Various," $2,100.00): *"Travel expenses for D. Hargrove and K. Cho to attend Dr. Rebecca Liu deposition — mileage, parking, meals."* The Case Status Report states that the Liu deposition "was taken at the offices of Hargrove & Linden LLP in Atlanta, Georgia." Hargrove and Cho therefore traveled to their **own firm's office** in Atlanta to attend a deposition held there. Section 6.4 provides that "[l]ocal travel within the Atlanta metropolitan area is not billable under any circumstances." Section 8.2 further renders meals non-reimbursable except during approved out-of-town travel, which did not occur. Separately, Section 8.3 requires pre-approval for any travel exceeding $1,000 in the aggregate, and no such approval appears in the record. The entire $2,100.00 is non-reimbursable. **Reduction: $2,100.00.**

**(b) Court reporting — $3,200.00 — approved vendor; verify expedited premium.** Expense 1 (Grayson Court Reporting Services, $3,200.00) covers the court reporter and transcript for the Liu deposition, "original transcript plus one copy, expedited delivery." Grayson is an approved vendor (Appendix B), and Section 8.1 reimburses "court reporter fees" and "deposition transcript costs (one copy per deposition, at standard rates)." The "expedited delivery" descriptor suggests a premium over standard rates. Pinnacle should require the underlying Grayson invoice and reduce any expedited surcharge that exceeds standard transcript rates. **No automatic reduction; flag for verification.**

**(c) Electronic discovery hosting — $2,650.00 — approved vendor; require vendor invoice.** Expense 3 (Tripoint Litigation Support LLC, $2,650.00) is monthly hosting for the ~85,000-document third production. Tripoint is the approved e-discovery vendor identified in the Approved Staffing Plan, and the amount is reasonable for that volume. Section 8.4 requires vendor costs to be "passed through at actual cost with no markup" and documented with the vendor invoice. **No reduction; require the Tripoint invoice to confirm pass-through at actual cost.**

**(d) Courier / delivery — $500.00 — verify time-sensitive justification and receipts.** Expense 4 ("Various," $500.00) covers "service of discovery documents and filing of motion to compel." Section 8.1 reimburses "messenger and courier services when time-sensitive delivery is required." Two concerns: (i) the motion to compel would have been filed electronically, which does not require a courier; and (ii) service on opposing counsel (Sternfield Morrison & Katz LLP, also in Atlanta) could ordinarily be accomplished by electronic or standard means. Pinnacle should require receipts and a time-sensitive justification; absent that, the $500.00 should be reduced. **No automatic reduction; flag for verification.**

**Recommendation.** Write off $2,100.00 (travel). Hold $3,700.00 (court reporting $3,200.00 + courier $500.00) pending vendor invoices and time-sensitive justification; pay the $2,650.00 Tripoint charge upon receipt of the vendor invoice confirming pass-through at actual cost. Recommended payable expenses: $6,350.00, of which $3,700.00 remains conditional on documentation.

### Finding 10 — Over-Lawyering / Redundant Senior Review (Section 6.2) — Observation

Section 6.2 prohibits "over-lawyering" — "multiple levels of attorney review of the same work product beyond what is reasonably necessary for quality control" — and provides that "[a] single round of review by the responsible supervising attorney is presumptively reasonable; additional rounds of review or involvement of additional senior timekeepers require justification." The invoice reflects a recurring pattern of three-level review that exceeds the presumptively reasonable single supervisory round:

- **Interrogatory responses:** drafted by Pettersson (Entries 2, 6, 11), reviewed and revised by Cho (Entries 3, 7, 12), and then re-edited by Hargrove on May 27 (Entry 92: "Review and edit interrogatory responses prepared by J. Pettersson"). That is a third review level, performed by the lead partner, with no stated justification.
- **Motion to compel:** drafted by Pettersson (Entries 19, 29), reviewed by Cho (Entries 20, 30, 35), and reviewed again by Hargrove (Entries 32, 43). Hargrove's Entry 43 cite-checking (Finding 6(a)) is particularly difficult to justify given that the approved junior associate was already cite-checking the same brief (Entry 47).
- **Deposition outlines (Liu and Krenshaw):** prepared by Pettersson, reviewed by Cho, and then conferenced with Hargrove, repeatedly.

No separate dollar reduction is assigned here because the redundant senior review is captured within the block-billed entries (Finding 4) and, in the case of Entry 43, the task-appropriateness reduction (Finding 6(a)). **Recommendation.** On resubmission, the Firm should either justify or remove the redundant senior review time. Going forward, Pinnacle should enforce the single-round-of-supervisory-review presumption and require justification for any additional senior review.

### Finding 11 — Insufficient Time Narrative (Section 9.2) — Observation

Section 9.2 requires narratives "sufficiently detailed to allow Pinnacle to assess the nature, necessity, and reasonableness of the work performed" and rejects generic descriptions such as "review documents." Most entries in this invoice are adequately described (Tate's document-review entries, for example, specify Bates ranges). The principal exception is Entry 79 (Webb): *"Review third batch of MedCore production documents for relevance and privilege"* — a generic description that does not identify the documents reviewed or the issues addressed, and that is indistinguishable from the routine first-level review the contract attorney was performing concurrently. This entry is reduced under Finding 6(b) for task appropriateness; if the Firm resubmits it, the narrative must be brought into Section 9.2 compliance. **Recommendation.** Reject any resubmitted entry that does not meet Section 9.2's specificity standard.

### Finding 12 — Billing Increments (Section 5.5) — Observation (Compliant)

Section 5.5 requires time in increments "of no greater than one-tenth (0.1) of an hour" and rejects quarter-hour (0.25) increments. All 117 entries are recorded in 0.5-hour multiples, which are valid 0.1-increment multiples; no quarter-hour (0.25) entries were found, so no Section 5.5 rounding reduction applies. Pinnacle should note, however, that the uniform 0.5-hour granularity is coarse and may overstate time on short tasks; the Firm should record time in true 0.1-hour increments going forward. **No reduction.**

### Finding 13 — Partner Hours Exceed the Approved Monthly Range — Observation

The Approved Staffing Plan projects 15–25 hours/month for David Hargrove. He billed 38.5 hours in May — 13.5 hours (54%) above the high end of the approved range. The Case Status Report acknowledges that "[m]r. Hargrove's involvement was significantly more extensive than in prior months" and predicts a return to typical levels. Exceeding an individual hour range is not, by itself, an automatic reduction under the Billing Guidelines (budget impact is governed by Section 7.3, addressed in Finding 8). The overage is nonetheless a flag: it helped drive the invoice over the Section 7.3 threshold, and a portion of the partner time was spent on tasks more appropriately delegated (document review, Entry 9; cite-checking, Entry 43). **No separate reduction; noted for budget-management follow-up.**

### Finding 14 — Rate-Cap Compliance for Approved Timekeepers — Observation (Compliant)

Apart from Marcus Tate (Finding 3), the approved timekeepers' rates are within the Section 5.1 caps: Hargrove $985 (cap $1,000); Cho $625 (cap $650); Pettersson $475 (cap $500); Webb $375 (cap $400). No reclassification of timekeepers (e.g., a contract attorney billed as a junior associate) was detected. **No reduction.**

## VI. Recommendations and Next Steps

1. **Do not pay the invoice as submitted.** Return Invoice No. HL-2025-05-PIN to Hargrove & Linden with the itemized reductions in this memorandum and a request that the Firm (a) reconcile the $22,307.50 / 61.0-hour internal discrepancy, (b) accept or contest the reductions in writing within thirty (30) days under Section 10.3, and (c) elect whether to cure the block-billed and task-misassigned entries by resubmission with separated time within fifteen (15) days under Sections 5.4 and 9.2.

2. **Authorize payment of no more than $147,136.88**, and hold $3,700.00 of that (court reporting and courier) pending receipt of the Grayson and courier vendor invoices and a time-sensitive justification. The $2,650.00 Tripoint charge should be paid upon receipt of the Tripoint invoice confirming pass-through at actual cost.

3. **Resolve the Barros and Sengupta staffing status in writing.** If Pinnacle wishes to add either or both prospectively, issue a written Section 4.2 approval (or denial) that specifies the approved rate, anticipated hours, and budget impact. # INVOICE REVIEW MEMORANDUM

**PRIVILEGED AND CONFIDENTIAL --- PREPARED IN ANTICIPATION OF BILLING DISPUTE RESOLUTION**

  ----------------------------------- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **To:**                             Rachel Dominguez, Associate General Counsel (Designated In-House Counsel); Sandra Whitmore, General Counsel

  **From:**                           Invoice Review / Legal Spend Management, Pinnacle Health Systems, Inc.

  **Date:**                           June 12, 2025

  **Re:**                             Review of Invoice No. HL-2025-05-PIN --- Hargrove & Linden LLP --- *Pinnacle Health Systems, Inc. v. MedCore Analytics, LLC*, Case No. 1:24-cv-04187-RWS (N.D. Ga.) --- Billing Period May 1--31, 2025

  **Invoice Amount Requested:**       \$201,822.50 (\$193,372.50 attorney fees + \$8,450.00 expenses)

  **Recommended Payable Amount:**     **\$147,136.88** (\$140,786.88 attorney fees + \$6,350.00 expenses)

  **Total Recommended Reduction:**    **\$54,685.62** from the amount requested (and \$76,993.12 from the gross line-item total)
  ----------------------------------- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

## I. Executive Summary

This memorandum reviews Invoice No. HL-2025-05-PIN, submitted by Hargrove & Linden LLP (the "Firm") on June 5, 2025 for legal services rendered during May 1--31, 2025 in the captioned matter. The review applies the Firm's Approved Discovery-Phase Staffing Plan (dated February 10, 2025), Pinnacle's Outside Counsel Billing Guidelines (effective January 1, 2023) (the "Billing Guidelines"), the April 28 / May 1, 2025 staffing correspondence, and the Firm's June 1, 2025 Monthly Case Status Report.

The invoice should not be paid as submitted. The review identifies **fourteen distinct issues**, the most serious of which are:

1.  **The invoice does not reconcile internally.** The Time Detail line items actually total **\$215,680.00 in attorney fees (483.5 hours)**, but the invoice's Summary page and the Time Detail "SUBTOTAL" row both state **\$193,372.50 (422.5 hours)** --- an unexplained discrepancy of **\$22,307.50 and 61.0 hours**. The invoice therefore fails the Section 9.1 requirement that the summary page and time detail page reconcile, and it cannot be processed until the Firm explains the variance.

2.  **Two timekeepers who billed the entire month were never approved.** Nina Barros (mid-level associate, 49.0 hours, \$23,275.00) and Priya Sengupta (junior associate, 27.0 hours, \$10,125.00) are not on the Approved Staffing Plan. The Firm's own Rate Schedule admits Barros is "Pending Approval" and Sengupta was simply "Added May 2025." The April 28 staffing email requested only Barros, and Pinnacle's May 1 reply expressly deferred approval ("let me review this with Sandra and get back to you"); no approval ever issued. Under Section 4.2, all fees attributable to unapproved timekeepers are subject to reduction or rejection --- a combined **\$33,400.00**.

3.  **The contract attorney was billed above both the approved rate and the rate cap.** Marcus Tate was billed at \$210/hr, exceeding both the Approved Staffing Plan rate of \$185/hr (pass-through, no markup) and the Section 5.1 cap of \$200/hr --- a **\$3,862.50** overcharge across 154.5 hours.

4.  **Block billing is pervasive.** Fifty-three (53) entries among the approved timekeepers combine two or more discrete tasks into a single line, triggering the automatic 25% reduction under Section 5.4 --- **\$30,220.62**.

5.  **A single internal strategy call was billed by five timekeepers and a key deposition by three, both in excess of the Section 4.3 limits** and without the required advance approval --- **\$3,312.50**.

6.  **Travel expense of \$2,100.00 is non-reimbursable.** It was incurred for two attorneys to travel to the Firm's own Atlanta office for a deposition taken there; local travel within the Atlanta metropolitan area is not billable under any circumstances (Section 6.4).

After applying all reductions to the line-item fee base of \$215,680.00, the recommended payable attorney fees are **\$140,786.88**. After expense review, recommended payable expenses are **\$6,350.00** (with an additional \$3,700.00 flagged for verification). The **recommended total payable is \$147,136.88**, a reduction of **\$54,685.62** from the \$201,822.50 requested.

The invoice should be returned to the Firm with this memorandum's itemized reductions, with payment of \$147,136.88 (at most) held pending (a) reconciliation of the \$22,307.50 internal discrepancy, (b) documentation for the \$3,700.00 of flagged expenses, and (c) the Firm's election to resubmit block-billed and task-misassigned entries with separated time under the cure provisions of Sections 5.4 and 9.2.

After applying all reductions to the line-item fee base of $215,680.00, the recommended payable attorney fees are **$140,786.88**. After expense review, recommended payable expenses are **$6,350.00** (with an additional $3,700.00 flagged for verification). The **recommended total payable is $147,136.88**, a reduction of **$54,685.62** from the $201,822.50 requested.

## IV. Findings and Reduction Calculations

### Finding 1 — Unapproved Timekeeper: Nina Barros (Section 4.2) — $23,275.00

Nina Barros, a fourth-year mid-level associate, is not on the Approved Staffing Plan, which states "James Pettersson is the only mid-level associate assigned to this matter. No other mid-level associate is included on the approved staffing roster." On April 28, 2025, Karen Cho emailed Rachel Dominguez requesting Barros's addition at "$475/hr" for "40–50 hours per month." Pinnacle's Designated In-House Counsel did **not** approve. Rachel Dominguez's May 1, 2025 reply states verbatim: *"Before we can move forward on adding Nina Barros, though, let me review this with Sandra and get back to you. I also want to check where things stand against the current budget and staffing plan on our end. I'll try to circle back with you early next week."* No subsequent written approval appears in the record. The Firm's own Rate Schedule concedes the point, listing Barros as "Pending Approval — Added May 2025" with the note "Requested via email April 28, 2025; client response pending." Notwithstanding the absence of approval, Barros billed 49.0 hours ($23,275.00) across fifteen entries. Section 4.2: *"Staffing changes submitted after the fact, or where the new timekeeper has already commenced work prior to receiving written approval, are subject to reduction or rejection of all fees attributable to the unapproved timekeeper, at Pinnacle's sole discretion."* The Firm did not invoke the exigent-circumstances exception (no 24-hour notice; the production and deposition schedule were foreseeable). **Recommendation:** write off all $23,275.00; require a complete Section 4.2 request before any prospective retention.

### Finding 2 — Unapproved Timekeeper: Priya Sengupta (Section 4.2) — $10,125.00

Priya Sengupta, a first-year junior associate, is not on the Approved Staffing Plan, which authorizes a single junior-associate slot (then occupied by Tyler Webb) and states verbatim: *"only one junior associate will be assigned to and authorized to bill on this matter at any given time."* Sengupta's addition as a **second** concurrent junior associate is a staffing change requiring pre-approval. Unlike Barros, Sengupta was not even the subject of a staffing request — the April 28 email requested only Barros. The Firm's Rate Schedule lists her status only as "Added May 2025." She billed 27.0 hours ($10,125.00) across eleven entries. To the extent her early entries (e.g., Entry 31, May 9: "Participate in litigation team strategy discussion; begin research…") reflect onboarding, Section 6.3 renders "getting up to speed" time non-billable except for a single two-hour briefing. **Recommendation:** write off all $10,125.00.

### Finding 3 — Contract Attorney Rate Exceeds Both the Approved Rate and the Cap (Sections 5.1 and 4.4) — $3,862.50

Marcus Tate (contract attorney via Redwood Document Solutions, an approved vendor) was billed at **$210/hr**. This violates two independent limits: (a) the Approved Staffing Plan rate of **$185/hr** ("pass-through at vendor rates with no firm markup"), confirmed in Section 6 of the Plan; and (b) the Section 5.1 cap of **$200/hr** for "Contract Attorney / Staff Attorney" ("[r]ates in excess of these caps will be automatically reduced to the applicable cap"). The correct rate is the approved $185/hr. At 154.5 line-item hours, the overcharge is 154.5 × ($210 − $185) = **$3,862.50**. **Recommendation:** reduce Tate's rate to $185/hr and request the underlying Redwood invoice to confirm $185 is the true vendor pass-through cost with no markup (Section 4.4).

### Finding 4 — Block Billing (Section 5.4) — $30,220.62

Section 5.4 prohibits entries that "combine two or more discrete tasks into a single line item" and requires "an automatic twenty-five percent (25%) reduction to the total fees of each block-billed entry," cumulative with other reductions. Block billing is pervasive. Fifty-three entries among the approved timekeepers combine distinct, independently timeable activities. Representative examples: Entry 2 (Pettersson): *"Draft responses to MedCore's Second Set of Interrogatories (Nos. 12–18); research Georgia Trade Secrets Act preemption…"*; Entry 12 (Cho): *"Final review and revision of all interrogatory responses; prepare cover letter for service on opposing counsel; coordinate filing deadlines"*; Entry 61 (Cho): *"Prepare for and attend deposition of Dr. Rebecca Liu…; post-deposition analysis and team debrief"*; Entry 92 (Hargrove): *"Prepare for and conduct strategy session…; draft outline for expert discovery plan. Review and edit interrogatory responses prepared by J. Pettersson."*
